result:
# Static

The radio in my grandmother's kitchen picked up a station from another country on clear nights. We never knew which country. The voices came through warped and warm, speaking languages that sounded like they were made of water, and she would stand at the sink with her hands still in the soapy water, just listening, not pretending to understand.

She said it didn't matter what they were saying. *The fact that they're saying it,* she told me once, *is enough.*

I think about that more than I probably should.

---

There's a particular quality of light in late October that I've never been able to describe to anyone's satisfaction, including my own. It's not golden. Golden is too simple, too flattering. It's more like the light is tired. Like it's been traveling all year and it's finally admitting something. The shadows go long and lean, and everything they fall across looks briefly historical, like you could be standing in any decade, any century, just a person in the fading light of a late afternoon, watching the same tired light do the same tired beautiful thing.

My father called it *the dying light* but not in a sad way. In the way you'd say *the ocean* or *the mountains.* Just naming something that was bigger than you.

---

I once drove seventeen hours straight because I didn't want to stop. Not because I had somewhere to be. I was moving between apartments, between versions of myself, and stopping felt like it would require me to acknowledge where I was, and I wasn't ready to be anywhere specific. I ate chips from a gas station bag. I drank terrible coffee. I watched the landscape change from green to flat to dry to mountainous and back to flat again, and somewhere in Iowa at 3am, with the road absolutely empty and the stars doing their impossible work overhead, I felt something I've never been able to name.

Not happiness. Not peace, exactly.

More like *permission.*

---

My grandmother died on a Tuesday in March. The radio in her kitchen was still there when we cleaned out the house. My cousin tried to throw it away and I stopped her, and then I couldn't explain why, and then I just held it for a long time in the kitchen with the afternoon coming through the window, and I didn't turn it on because I didn't want to find out it only picked up local stations now.

Some things are better kept possible.

---

I've been thinking lately about how much of what we call *self* is just accumulated habit. The way you take your coffee. Which side of the bed. The particular anxious gesture you make with your hands when you're explaining something you're not sure about. At some point these things weren't yours. You acquired them, or they acquired you, through repetition and accident and proximity to other people who were also just accumulating their own habits and calling it a personality.

None of this is a complaint.

I find it comforting, actually. It means the self is more porous than we think. It means you've been shaped by gas station breakfasts and the wrong turn you took in a city you didn't know and the song that came on the radio when you were seventeen and feeling things so large you were sure your body wasn't big enough to contain them.

It means other people are in you. Woven in. Whether you asked them or not.

---

My grandmother spoke to the radio voices the way she spoke to birds. Not expecting an answer. Just acknowledging that something was alive out there, making its noise, doing its thing, carrying on.

I think that's a kind of faith. Not the organized kind. The older kind. The kind that preceded buildings and doctrines and required nothing except your willingness to stand still and let yourself be briefly astonished by the fact that the world is full of voices traveling enormous distances through the dark just to come through scratchy and warm in someone's kitchen while the dishes soak.

---

It's late now where I am.

The specific quality of quiet that exists at this hour is also something I've never managed to describe properly. It's not silent. There's always something. A car somewhere. The particular voice a house makes when the heat kicks on. Outside, depending on the season, there are crickets or wind or both or neither.

But the quality of it is different from daytime quiet. It feels *earned.* Like the day used itself up and this is what's left over. The residue of all those hours.

I find it easier to think in it. Easier to remember things. Easier to feel the weight of the years, which is not always comfortable but is always, in some way I can't quite get at, *good.*

---

My grandmother would have been a hundred and two this year.

Somewhere a radio is picking up a voice from another country. Someone is standing at a sink with their hands in warm water, not understanding the words, understanding everything else.

The light is doing what light does.

Something is alive out there, making its noise.

That's enough. It really is.
